title: SS19/13 – Resolution planning

chapters: Appendix: Reporting templates and instructions- MRL001-MRL003

boe_references.title: The Supervisory Statement (SS) shown on this page is effective from 01/01/2019. Past versions of this SS can be found here.

paragraphs.summary: The objective of this section is to provide the authorities with sufficient information to assess the preferred resolution strategy for the firm.
All firms are expected to provide an overview of their business model, internal dependencies and external interconnectedness.

Small firms should provide information relevant to their size and structure. Larger firms should provide information relating to their significant legal entities. Although firms are not expected to pr

Information provided should include material global activities (both where these are material to the firm and where the firm is significant in overseas markets) and describe the global links and inter
Where a firm’s parent organisation is a UK-incorporated entity, firms should provide information relating to all significant group entities (subsidiaries and branches).
Where a firm’s parent organisation is incorporated outside the United Kingdom (eg foreign firms with UK subsidiaries) firms should provide information relating to:

The objective of this section is to identify which of a firm’s economic functions may be critical to the financial system, either domestically or internationally. This will help the authorities to dev
Information should be provided by legal entity. Market share information should be provided for both domestic market shares as well as local overseas market shares where such overseas activity may be
The purpose of this table is to map the economic functions performed by firms to their legal entities, based on the analysis above.

Where a firm’s parent organisation is a UK-incorporated entity, firms should complete this table for all significant legal entities and branches that form part of the group, both domestically and inte
Where a firm’s parent organisation is incorporated outside the United Kingdom, firms should only complete this table for:

The overall purpose for the collection of information on economic activities and mapping them to legal entities is to inform the authorities’ selection of a preferred resolution strategy for a firm. T

The information outlined below will be requested from firms to facilitate the development of a resolution plan based on the use of the bail-in tool and to monitor firms’ compliance with MREL and their

The information will be used to identify the barriers to using a bail-in resolution strategy; where further work is required to overcome barriers; and to develop a detailed plan to effect the transact
The information will also provide a baseline for potential further work relating to the continuity of the firm’s critical economic functions during a bail-in.
The information requested may include the following:
This information will permit resolution authorities to:
Firms may be required to provide information on all unsecured intra-group and external loans or debt securities in issue. This includes subordinated and senior debt and privately placed issuances.
As part of the information on loss-absorbing capacity (section A1.1), firms should also provide informa
The following information may be required for each instrument:
Firms may also be required to submit:
Firms may be required to provide the following details about the contractual terms of their debt instruments:
Firms may be required to provide the following information relating to intra-group LAC:
Firms may be required to provide the following information relating to their MI systems:
Deleted.

Groups will be required to quantify business as usual (twelve-month average) intra-group exposures between legal entities (branch or subsidiary). This will allow the authorities to assess the feasibil
The analysis should cover the entities of the group that are material from a funding flow perspective.
For groups where the preferred group resolution is bail-in at the level of UK parent company, the assessment should identify intra-group flows between the UK entity and all significant legal entities
For non-UK groups, where the preferred group resolution strategy is a bail-in led by overseas resolution authorities, the assessment will seek to identify intra-group flows that involve UK entities.

Firms should submit information across products such as money market funding, debt instruments, secured transactions, collateral alignment, derivatives, off balance sheet commitments and guarantees. E

The tables below are templates for summary information on intra-group exposures. However, supervisors will guide firms on the form of information to be provided as part of bilateral work (the template
In order to ensure that key services can be maintained in resolution, and that contracts prevent termination in resolution, firms are expected to provide contract documentation for service agreements

Firms should describe their system for safekeeping legal documents and explain how easy it is to extract information from this system. Firms are expected to set out the firm’s proposed approach to ali
Quantify the scale of the firm’s service agreement documentation, providing a breakdown by:

For economically material contracts firms should provide details of key clauses such as break/insolvency, services, costs, encumbrances. Firms should make clear where these relate to intra-group agree
Quantify the scale of the firm’s trading documentation, including general breakdown by:
For economically material contracts:
Quantify the scale of the firm’s guarantees, including general breakdown by:
For economically material contracts:
Set out the firm’s proposed approach for aligning contract documentation (standardised and non-standardised) to facilitate the preferred resolution strategy.

The information provided in this section will allow the authorities to understand any critical internal or external operational shared services that may hinder resolution and affect post-resolution bu

An indicative list of the information that firms should submit is listed below. Where critical dependencies may represent a barrier to the execution of a post bail-in business reorganisation, firms sh

The information outlined below will enable the authorities to develop a resolution plan based on the use of stabilisation transfer tools under the Banking Act 2009 – that is a whole or partial transfe

Information provided by firms will enable the authorities to assess the feasibility of grouping and separating the firm’s functions into viable business units. It will facilitate the identification of

The information provided in this section will facilitate the identification of the different options for grouping the firm’s assets and liabilities to be saleable or transferable to a third party or b
Information provided in this section will enable the authorities to understand any critical internal or external operational shared services that may hinder resolution and post-resolution bridge bank

Firms are expected to assess the level of continuity of critical functions provided when executing a transfer of each of the packages of assets and liabilities (described in [Section A2.1](/guidance/s

Examples of barriers that the firm should address include activities that span legal jurisdictions, associated derivatives exposures and netting arrangements, or operational/financial/structural idios

Firms should also provide information on MREL resources, projected resources, and individual instrument characteristics as set out in [section A4](/guidance/supervisory-statements/ss19-13-resolution-p

Most of the information required to assess the feasibility of executing a Bank Insolvency Procedure is already provided to the authorities through business as usual regulatory reporting and the inform

The PRA requires firms to be able to produce a single, consistent view of each eligible depositor’s funds, to enable the FSCS to implement rapid payout in the event of a resolution. We require firms a
However firms may be required to submit additional information, for example, if they undertake a specific economic function that may present particular complications in resolution. In this case firms

The PRA expects firms that have been notified by the Bank of England that they are, or are likely to be, subject to external or internal MREL in excess of regulatory capital requirements to provide in
The PRA expects firms to submit information on MREL resources as set out in the templates in the appendix of this statement. The names and contents of the templates are as follows:
The PRA expects firms to report at the following frequencies.
The following table shows the level of application and frequency of MREL reporting:
Firms should provide information in the XBRL format using the templates in the appendix.
The PRA expects firms to commence reporting at the following times.

The following section sets out additional information and analysis that may be requested from firms in order to further assess the feasibility of a particular resolution strategy, identify barriers to

Firms should consider the need to maintain continuity in the critical economic functions that they provide as well as the need to limit contagion and disruption to customers counterparties and other m

The authorities’ need for further information will depend on the complexity of the individual firm and its preferred resolution strategy. Additional information requested may include, but is not limit
This section focuses on the Financial Market Infrastructure (FMI) which are critical to the firm and customers with which it has a correspondent banking relationship.
This section focuses on the trading book, with particular focus on the firm’s booking model, trade documentation and operational continuity of the trading book in a resolution.
This section focuses on the scope and scale of the cash services business line, and operational continuity of these operations in a resolution.
This section focuses on the scope and scale of custody services (including ancillary services such as securities lending), as well as funding and operational continuity in resolution.
To facilitate resolution planning the authorities may require additional information on the following:

Following intervention using the bail-in resolution tool (introduced by the Banking Reform Bill) the Bank of England may require a bail-in administrator or one or more directors of the bank under reso

The reorganisation plan will need to consider a range of options. These may include: (i) options for maintaining the continuity of core functions; (ii) ease of unwind of core functions, and (iii) ease

To facilitate the timely production of a reorganisation plan in the event of resolution, the PRA may require a firm to consider in advance a range of restructuring options that might present an effect
This section focuses on operational continuity in a resolution.
This section focuses on the operational continuity in a resolution for firms that have significant service company models.

In order to plan for resolution and possible restructuring the authorities will need to understand firms’ business as usual funding requirements in more detail than their current liquidity reporting p
The template on page 38 provides an example of the information that firms may be required to provide, a

A fundamental part of resolution planning and executing resolution powers is for the authorities to conduct a robust valuation of a firm. This valuation is designed to identify equity value, quantum o
The assessment of a firm’s valuation systems and controls, processes and resources will form part of business-as-usual supervision to ensure firms are resolvable in the event of failure. This section

As firms experience stress, and approach possible resolution, they will come under increased scrutiny. They should anticipate that the authorities will need additional, or more up-to-date, information

In preparation for a firm’s potential entry into resolution, the authorities may need additional information to facilitate protection of the firm’s deposit-taking function. This may include (among oth
Firms may be expected to provide up-to-date information on holders of debt liabilities (to the extent possible) to assess loss-absorbing capacity.
Firms will be expected to provide up-to-date liquidity information to support contingency planning.

During the resolution or recovery process, there may be circumstances in which the Bank considers it necessary to provide liquidity to firms, secured against their assets. Firms should be able to supp
In preparation for potential resolution, the authorities may need more detailed information relating to the cash services function the firm provides, including the information below.
